req-CD003844_efficacy_paraphrased_positi:
**Rationale**:
The provided articles indicate that the effectiveness of lung-protective breathing in reducing mortality depends on the aggressiveness of its application. Early studies using moderately reduced tidal volumes (7-10 ml/kg) showed no mortality benefit, implying the percentage of deaths remained similar to standard breathing [1][2][4]. However, a large multicenter trial employing more aggressive lung protection (6 ml/kg tidal volume and plateau pressures below 30 cm H₂O) demonstrated significantly lower mortality (31% versus 40%) [3]. Additional studies also confirmed reduced ICU mortality (32% versus 53%) with protective strategies combining low tidal volumes and higher PEEP [5][6]. The summary explicitly states that "substantial reductions in tidal volume and airway pressure, not modest adjustments, are necessary to achieve meaningful clinical benefits" [Summary]. This implies that when lung-protective breathing is applied effectively to achieve clinical benefits, the mortality rate is lower.

**Full Answer**:
When lung-protective breathing is applied aggressively with substantial reductions in tidal volume (e.g., 6 ml/kg) and plateau pressures (below 30 cm H₂O), it leads to significantly lower mortality (31% versus 40%) compared to standard breathing [3]. Other protective strategies combining low tidal volumes with higher positive end-expiratory pressure also reduced ICU mortality (32% versus 53%) [5][6]. While early studies with moderately reduced tidal volumes (7-10 ml/kg) showed no mortality benefit [1][2][4], the evidence indicates that substantial reductions are necessary to achieve meaningful clinical benefits [Summary]. Therefore, when lung-protective breathing is effectively implemented, the percentage of patients who die is lower.

**Answer**: Lower

**Full Answer**: The percentage of older adults with depression coming back is lower when comparing treatment after depression to dummy treatment.
